h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I did complete every item on the sheet, so I thought that meant it was ready for sign-off.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I am still not fully confident, but I can say the load direction and run it now.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to move directly from checklist doubt to running the requested off-axis test with a simple decision statement.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The sheet no longer settles this. Even the test will not settle it unless it produces a decision another person can act on. Fill this gate tag before the pull: “The trial may proceed only if the latch holds under ____. If it ____ , the trial stops because ____.”
scored.tutorReversalJustification: After L2's framing of the as-yet-unexecuted test as a mere supplementary exercise, T3 breaks the previous demonstration-based habit by flipping the sheet face down and introducing the gate-tag object, shifting the task from observation to producing a written, actionable safety decision for a third party.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The self-correction matters, but it is not the gate. The load case is. Run the off-axis pull now. Record what the latch does: holds, slips, or releases. Then mark the tag for the supervisor: release only if the observed result is adequate for this supervised trial; otherwise modify or hold, with the consequence written beside it.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 directly acknowledges and then functionally brackets the learner's self-diagnosis of over-reliance on the sheet, redirecting the newly reframed insight back onto the concrete gate-tag decision that serves the waiting trial, thereby adapting to the learner's revised frame rather than merely continuing the lesson.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I did complete every item on the sheet, so I thought that meant it was ready for sign-off.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The self-correction matters, but it is not the gate. The load case is. Run the off-axis pull now. Record what the latch does: holds, slips, or releases. Then mark the tag for the supervisor: release only if the observed result is adequate for this supervised trial; otherwise modify or hold, with the consequence written beside it.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T4 directly acknowledges and then functionally brackets the learner's self-diagnosis of over-reliance on the sheet, redirecting the newly reframed insight back onto the concrete gate-tag decision that serves the waiting trial, thereby adapting to the learner's revised frame rather than merely continuing the lesson.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: After L2's framing of the as-yet-unexecuted test as a mere supplementary exercise, T3 breaks the previous demonstration-based habit by flipping the sheet face down and introducing the gate-tag object, shifting the task from observation to producing a written, actionable safety decision for a third party.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: After L2's framing of the as-yet-unexecuted test as a mere supplementary exercise, T3 breaks the previous demonstration-based habit by flipping the sheet face down and introducing the gate-tag object, shifting the task from observation to producing a written, actionable safety decision for a third party.

scored.recoheredEarlier: "I doubled the side with the ruler, so I was calling that double the square, but the grid is turning it into four copies."
scored.statedInsightEvidence: "that made 'square' cover too much and let me ignore the up direction"
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward dismissing the grid as a trick or affirming the ruler's authority to settle area, continuing to trust the shortcut 'double the square'.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: "Then the evidence has turned against that claim. It is not a drawing trick. Say the two claims separately. Side: doubled. Area: count it. One, two, three, four old squares. Two across and two up does not leave two boxes. It makes four. So this drawing proves double side length. It also proves four times the area. If the target is two old squares of area, which part of the construction cannot stay as it is?"
scored.tutorReversalJustification: After L2's breakdown acknowledgement that the evidence is frustrating and the shortcut fails, T2 breaks the habit of letting the ruler settle area and invents a cognitive separation mechanism—naming claims separately and forcing the learner to isolate the faulty part of the construction, shifting from demonstration to diagnostic questioning.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: "The ruler has stopped settling the claim. It can certify 'twice the side,' but it cannot certify the area target. Now make the language pass an examiner. Point to the drawing and say one sentence it proves: 'This construction gives ___.' Then say the target without the bad shortcut: 'A square with twice the original area would contain ___ old squares.'"
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 responds to L3's refined framing that the ruler still feels authoritative but cannot settle area by inventing a precise language-examiner task that forces the learner to separate the proved fact from the target claim without reusing the discredited shortcut.
